text.title: NFR Measurability and Acceptance Criteria Coverage
text.summary: NFR measurability rules, acceptance criteria coverage matrix, and agent-specific antipatterns for both.

rule.statement: Every NFR must specify: quantifiable target, measurement approach (how to measure), and validation method (how to test). NFRs without quantifiable targets must be rejected and rewritten before spec approval.
rule.rationale: "System should be fast" is not an NFR — it is a preference. Without a number, neither developers nor agents can know when the requirement is met.

example.code: ### NFR-001: Add to Cart Latency **Requirement:** Add to cart operation SHALL complete in < 200ms at p95. **Measurement:** Client-side total time from click to UI update (Performance API). **Priority:** Must **Validation:** Load test with 500 concurrent users, measure p95 latency.

antipattern.description: Agent writes NFR as "The system should handle high traffic" or "Response times should be acceptable".
antipattern.reason: LLMs produce qualitative NFR language because they lack knowledge of the specific system's performance profile. Require the agent to propose a specific number and explicitly mark it [estimate] — then the human validates or corrects the target.

rule.statement: For each functional requirement, acceptance criteria must cover: happy path, at least one error case, at least one edge case (boundary condition or empty state). For security-sensitive FRs, add an unauthorized access scenario.
rule.rationale: Specs with only happy path AC produce features that break on first error condition in production. The coverage matrix forces specification of failure behavior before implementation begins.

rule.statement: Use Given-When-Then format for all acceptance criteria. Avoid free-form prose AC. "Works correctly" is not an acceptance criterion.
rule.rationale: Given-When-Then enforces testability. Each field maps directly to a test: Given = test setup, When = action, Then = assertion. Prose AC requires interpretation, causing inconsistent test coverage.

antipattern.description: AC covers only the happy path for all FRs, with no error or edge cases.
antipattern.reason: Agents default to positive-case thinking. Explicitly require error scenarios for every FR that involves user input, external calls, or state transitions. Ask: "What happens when this fails?"

rule.statement: Document every assumption that, if wrong, would require a spec change. Format: "Assumption: [statement]. If wrong: [consequence]."
rule.rationale: Hidden assumptions are the primary source of mid-development spec surprises. Making the "if wrong" consequence explicit accelerates discovery of invalid assumptions during review.
